thesis: Tiny, repeatable improvements compound into remarkable outcomes when they are attached to identity, supported by systems, and made obvious, attractive, easy, and satisfying.
framework: Cue, craving, response, and reward become the habit loop. The Four Laws reshape that loop: make it obvious, attractive, easy, and satisfying.
overview: Atomic Habits argues that outcomes are lagging measures of systems. Durable change starts by improving the system that produces behavior.
The deepest layer of change is identity. Instead of asking what result you want, ask who you want to become and cast votes for that identity through repeated action.
Environment design matters because disciplined people often build surroundings that make good behavior easier and bad behavior harder.

learningModes.label: Flash
learningModes.title: Learn in 1 Minute
learningModes.summary: The book is a practical system for making small behaviors compound.

sections.title: Book Thesis
items: Small habits compound into large life changes.
Identity-based habits outlast outcome-based goals.
Systems beat motivation because they shape default behavior.

sections.title: Top Lessons
items: Make good habits obvious, attractive, easy, and satisfying.
Make bad habits invisible, unattractive, difficult, and unsatisfying.
Never miss twice; protect the identity even after a slip.

sections.title: Who Should Read It
items: Anyone trying to build a routine.
People who repeatedly set goals but lose momentum.
Leaders designing behavior-friendly environments.

learningModes.label: Core
learningModes.title: Learn in 10 Minutes
learningModes.summary: Understand the habit loop and the Four Laws of Behavior Change.

sections.title: Core Framework
items: Cue creates awareness.
Craving creates desire.
Response is the behavior.
Reward teaches the brain to repeat it.

sections.title: Main Concepts
items: Identity-based habits
Habit stacking
Environment design
Two-minute rule
Habit tracking

sections.title: Key Examples
items: British Cycling improved many small details until the gains compounded.
A habit scorecard exposes invisible behaviors.
Putting a guitar in the middle of the room makes practice more likely.

learningModes.label: Deep
learningModes.title: Learn in 30 Minutes
learningModes.summary: Connect the book's mental models, stories, examples, and actions.

sections.title: Detailed Understanding
items: Outcomes come from systems, systems come from repeated behavior, and repeated behavior comes from identity and environment.
The plateau of latent potential explains why progress feels delayed before visible results appear.
Behavior is easier to change when the desired action is the path of least resistance.

sections.title: Mental Models
items: Compounding
Feedback loops
Friction
Identity voting
Inversion

sections.title: Action Items
items: Choose one identity and one daily vote for it.
Design your room, desk, or phone to make the cue obvious.
Track the habit and recover immediately after misses.

learningModes.label: Library
learningModes.title: Full Depth
learningModes.summary: Explore the complete generated knowledge package.

sections.title: Includes
items: Overview, journey, contents, chapters, concepts, examples, actions, and source reading.
Chapter-level summaries preserve stories, examples, and practical takeaways.
Concept-first learning lets users move through the ideas without following page order.

journey.title: Identity
journey.description: Decide who you want to become before choosing the habit.

journey.title: Systems
journey.description: Build repeatable defaults that make the desired behavior easier.

journey.title: Habit Loop
journey.description: Shape cue, craving, response, and reward.

journey.title: Environment
journey.description: Make cues visible and remove friction from good actions.

journey.title: Compounding
journey.description: Let small improvements accumulate until outcomes become visible.

contents.title: Part 1: The Fundamentals
chapters: The Surprising Power of Atomic Habits
How Your Habits Shape Your Identity
How to Build Better Habits in 4 Simple Steps

contents.title: Part 2: Make It Obvious
chapters: The Man Who Didn't Look Right
The Best Way to Start a New Habit
Motivation Is Overrated; Environment Often Matters More

contents.title: Part 3: Make It Attractive, Easy, and Satisfying
chapters: How to Make a Habit Irresistible
The Cardinal Rule of Behavior Change

chapters.title: The Surprising Power of Atomic Habits
chapters.summary: Small improvements are easy to dismiss in the moment but become meaningful through compounding.
examples: British Cycling's aggregation of marginal gains
actionItems: Pick one tiny behavior that can be repeated daily

chapters.title: How Your Habits Shape Your Identity
chapters.summary: Identity-based change asks users to become the type of person who performs the behavior.
examples: A person who sees themselves as a reader makes reading a default behavior
actionItems: Write the identity behind one goal

chapters.title: How to Build Better Habits in 4 Simple Steps
chapters.summary: The habit loop can be designed through four laws and inverted to break unwanted habits.
examples: Habit stacking attaches a new action to an existing cue
actionItems: Create one habit stack using: After I do X, I will do Y

concepts.title: Identity-Based Habits
concepts.description: Behavior change that starts with the person you want to become.
concepts.whyItMatters: Identity keeps habits from depending only on mood, goals, or deadlines.

concepts.title: The Four Laws
concepts.description: Make it obvious, attractive, easy, and satisfying.
concepts.whyItMatters: The laws turn habit formation into a design problem.

concepts.title: Environment Design
concepts.description: Arrange surroundings so cues and friction guide behavior.
concepts.whyItMatters: The room often wins against willpower.

concepts.title: Two-Minute Rule
concepts.description: Scale a new habit down until it can be started in two minutes.

examples.authorExample: British Cycling improved many small inputs until the combined advantage became decisive.
examples.story: The team focused on tiny optimizations across training, equipment, recovery, and routine.
examples.modernExample: A learner reads two pages daily, then compounds vocabulary, focus, and confidence.
examples.personalApplication: Choose one improvement small enough to repeat without negotiation.

examples.authorExample: Keeping a guitar visible makes practice more likely than storing it in a closet.
examples.story: The cue becomes part of the room instead of an item that must be remembered.
examples.modernExample: Place the book on your pillow after making the bed.
examples.personalApplication: Move one desired habit cue into your natural line of sight.

examples.authorExample: A visual chain makes repetition satisfying and motivates consistency.
examples.story: The tracker gives immediate feedback before long-term results arrive.
examples.modernExample: A simple calendar mark after every study session.
examples.personalApplication: Track one habit for seven days and protect the streak from two misses.

tomorrow: Write one identity statement: I am becoming the kind of person who...
Create one habit stack for a two-minute action.
Put the cue for the habit somewhere visible.
thisWeek: Run a habit scorecard across one normal day.
Remove one source of friction from a good habit.
Add one point of friction to a habit you want to reduce.
thisMonth: Review weekly habit data and adjust the environment.
Add one slightly larger version of the habit after consistency is stable.
Pair the habit with a reward that reinforces the identity.
